data.title: Rwanda received migrants deported from the US earlier this month
data.description:
Rwanda received seven migrants deported from the United States earlier this month, the government spokesperson said in a statement on Thursday, weeks after the two countries reached an agreement for the transfer of up to 250 people.

"The first group of seven vetted migrants arrived in Rwanda in mid-August," Rwandan government spokesperson Yolande Makolo said in a statement.

"Three of the individuals have expressed a desire to return to their home countries, while four wish to stay and build lives in Rwanda. Regardless of their specific needs, all of these individuals will receive appropriate support and protection from the Rwandan government."

The U.S. Department of Homeland Security and State Department did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

U.S. President Donald Trump has taken a hardline approach toward immigration, aiming to deport millions of immigrants in the U.S. illegally and seeking to ramp up removals to third countries.

In early August, Rwanda and the United States entered an agreement for Rwanda to take in up to 250 migrants, with Washington sending to Rwanda an initial list of 10 people to be vetted.

Rwanda has in recent years positioned itself as a destination country for migrants that Western nations would like to remove, despite concerns by rights groups that Kigali does not respect basic human rights.

The Trump administration says that third-country deportations help quickly remove some migrants, including those with criminal convictions. Immigration hardliners see third-country removals as a way to deal with offenders who cannot easily be deported and could pose a threat to the public.

Opponents have criticized the deportations as dangerous and cruel, since people could be sent to countries where they could face violence, have no ties and do not speak the language.

Makolo said earlier this month that under the agreement reached with Washington, migrants deported to Rwanda would be provided with workforce training, healthcare, and accommodation.

On Thursday, Makolo said that those deported to Rwanda were accompanied by an international organization and receiving visits by the International Organization for Migration and Rwandan social services.

data.title: Valterra Platinum plans to start underground trial mining in late 2026
data.description:
Valterra Platinum will start trial mining at an underground pit at its Mogalakwena open-pit mine in South Africa late next year, an executive said on Wednesday.

Mogalakwena in Limpopo province, north of Johannesburg, is the world's largest open-pit platinum group metals (PGM) mine, comprising five open pits. The ore body of its Sandsloot pit, where the underground mine is being developed, has higher grades of ore.

Platinum miners in South Africa have faced declining ore grades for years. To keep production going, companies have had to mine deeper underground, which makes operations more expensive and risky.

The mine is the crown jewel of Valterra's portfolio, contributing about 50% of the company's PGM production. The project to develop the Sandsloot underground mine is a nod of confidence in the future of PGMs, used primarily in autocatalysts and jewellery.

Prices of the white metals have mostly slid in the past two years, with demand hit by the rise of battery electric vehicles, which don't require them, leading miners to cut supply.

In July, the company said it has begun the feasibility study for the underground project with a targeted completion and investment decision in the first half of 2027.

"We're also envisioning a trial mining ...in the back end of 2026," Martin Poggiolini, executive head of corporate strategy told reporters during a media tour of the mine on Wednesday.

"If it meets our capital allocation we will be able to truck the first ore out of the top part of the ore body to the surface," Poggiolini said, adding that ramping up to a 3.6 to a 4.5 million tonne a year operation was possible beyond 2030.

With the underground mine, Valterra hopes to achieve its targeted 10% to 50% overall increase in Mogalakwena concentrate production, it said in July.

Poggiolini and Agit Singh, Valterra's head of processing operations, said the company is scaling up demand across existing markets such as hydrogen production and fuel-cell electric vehicles and creating demand in new segments.

"So we have really good confidence in the fundamentals around the PGM sector in the short term and certainly in the longer term," Singh said.

data.title: Urban Outfitters Is Maintained at Overweight by Morgan Stanley
data.description: (16:20 GMT) Urban Outfitters Price Target Raised to $84.00/Share From $82.00 by Morgan Stanley

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1205 ET - Pure Storage is now on track to accelerate growth this year and beyond after seeing significant revenue momentum in its latest quarter, helped by broad-based strength across its portfolio, J.P. Morgan analysts say in a note. The company's raised guidance reflects its growing confidence in customers' spending intentions as the underlying macroeconomic environment remains more resilient than feared earlier in the year, the analysts say. Pure Storage's revenue performance and boosted outlook stand out relative to peers with more modest upsides and consistent outlooks, they say, noting such competitors are more exposed to a pullback in public sector spending. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)Delivery Hero reported first-half results. "Delivery Hero's Adjusted Earnings Guidance Cut Likely to Disappoint Investors — Market Talk," at 1043 GMT, misstated the company name.

0728 ET - Delivery Hero's results show solid growth and improving profitability, Mwb Research's Alexander Zienkowicz writes in a note. Regional trends have improved since the first quarter, with Asia rebounding as Foodpanda returned to growth and Woowa's in-house delivery expanded. Meanwhile, the Americas sustained double-digit top-line growth and Ebitda turned positive, he says. Operational momentum remains strong, cash flow and the company's balance sheet have strengthened, he adds. The only setback was foreign exchange rate headwinds, which led to a cut in full-year adjusted Ebitda guidance. Shares are down 1.9% at 23 euros. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0642 ET - Delivery Hero reported solid first-half results but the cut to 2025 adjusted Ebitda guidance is likely to weigh on shares, Citi analysts write in a note. The downgrade sets adjusted Ebitda at between 900 million euros and 940 million euros, down from the previously guided range of 975 million euros and 1.025 billion euros. This implies a 16% shortfall versus consensus, they say. Shares are down 1.9% at 22.99 euros.(najat.kantouar@wsj.com) Corrections & Amplifications

This article was corrected at 08:03 a.m. ET to fix the spelling of the company name.

0516 ET - Delivery Hero's cut to its 2025 adjusted Ebitda guidance raises questions, UBS analysts write in a note. The German food-delivery company now expects adjusted Ebitda to be in the range of 900 million euros and 940 million euros, down from 975 million euros to 1.025 billion euros, citing foreign exchange rate headwinds. This exchange rate pressure was acknowledged earlier in the year, so the reason driving the downgrade is unclear, the analysts add. The group's solid first-half results add to this uncertainty, they say. Shares are down 0.8% at 23.25 euros. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0509 ET - Nvidia's outlook for the current quarter is underwhelming since it doesn't include sales of H20 artificial-intelligence chips to China, Kathleen Brooks, research director at XTB, writes in a note. The U.S. restricted sales of H20 chips to China in April, costing Nvidia billions of dollars. However, Nvidia received assurances from the Trump administration last month that it could sell them again. For the current quarter, Nvidia expects overall sales of $54 billion, plus or minus 2%, assuming no shipments of H20 chips to China. "Without clear guidance on China revenues, it is hard to see what the next driver of Nvidia's share price will be," Brooks says. Nvidia shares are down 1.6% premarket at $178.77. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0450 ET - The fall in Nvidia's stock price Wednesday aftermarket and Thursday premarket indicates that investors are disappointed on the China front. The tech giant didn't sell any H20 artificial-intelligence chips to China-based customers in the fiscal second quarter ended July 27 even though the Trump administration lifted export restrictions last month. "The longer it takes to generate sales in China, the more likely it is that Chinese consumers will turn to domestic chip makers who could eventually rival Nvidia's chips for their advanced capabilities," Kathleen Brooks, research director at XTB, writes in a note to clients. Nvidia shares are down 1.5% premarket at $178.83. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0449 ET - PPF's decision to sell its stake in German broadcaster ProSiebenSat.1 Media supports consolidation efforts, Equita analyst Milo Silvestre writes in a note. The Czech investment conglomerate is tendering around 15.7% of ProSiebenSat.1's share capital into Netherlands-based media broadcasting group MFE-MediaForEurope's $2.4 billion takeover offer. PPF's participation in the takeover bid is supportive for MFE's stock, giving MFE control of ProSiebenSat.1 and removing uncertainty over the asset's future, he says. The consolidation is also strategically sound, strengthening MFE's presence in one of Europe's largest free-to-air advertising markets, he adds. MFE and ProSiebenSat.1 shares rise 6.4% and 2.1%, respectively. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0435 ET - Nvidia shares edge lower Thursday premarket after sales at the tech giant's data-center business and guidance for the current quarter missed analysts' forecasts. Nvidia stock closed 0.1% lower at $181.60 Wednesday ahead of results. Shares are down 1.9% at $178.21 Thursday premarket. Sales from Nvidia's data-center business climbed 56% to $41.1 billion in the fiscal second quarter ended July 27, slightly below a FactSet estimate of $41.30 billion. For the current quarter, Nvidia expects overall sales of $54 billion, plus or minus 2%, assuming no shipments of H20 chips to China. Analysts expect sales of $54.06 billion for the quarter, according to FactSet. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)Chinese Shares reversed earlier losses to end higher. "Chinese Shares End Higher, Supported by Chip Stocks — Market Talk," at 0757 GMT, misstated that Chinese shares reversed earlier gains.

0407 ET - Bitcoin recovers modestly from recent losses as investors shrug off Nvidia's underwhelming earnings report, MH Markets analyst Mohammed Taha says. Risk sentiment is bolstered by expectations that the Federal Reserve will resume interest-rate cuts soon ahead of Friday's U.S. personal consumption expenditures price data, he says. A lower-than-expected print could reinforce hopes for a September rate cut, historically a tailwind for risky assets like bitcoin, he says. "Moreover, the U.S. strategic bitcoin reserve initiative, despite its limitations to forfeited assets, continues to signal long-term governmental support, sustaining institutional interest." Bitcoin rises 0.7% to $113,199, LSEG data show. Nvidia's shares fell in postclose trade after its second quarter results included a narrow revenue miss in a key data-center segment. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0406 ET - Delivery Hero reported mixed results, RBC Capital Markets analyst Wassachon Fon Udomsilpa writes in a note. The German food-delivery company's second-quarter gross merchandise value--the total value of all transactions made on the platform and a key metric in the industry-- came in slightly lower than consensus. However, first-half Ebitda came in ahead, she says. Meanwhile, the company raised its GMV and revenue growth guidance for the full-year, but lowered its Ebitda outlook due to foreign-exchange headwinds, she notes. The updated Ebitda guidance is about 7% below market expectations, she adds. Shares are down 1% at 23.20 euros.(najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

2058 ET - Private hospital operator Ramsay Health Care drops 13% to A$33.18, with RBC Capital Markets pointing to a hat-trick of negative news. Ramsay's Australian and U.K. earnings in FY 2025 were weaker than expected. Ramsay also pointed to a A$37 million hit to EBIT coming in FY 2026 from a new funding agreement that affects its Joondalup campus in Western Australia. Also included in its guidance are projected net financing costs of A$600 million-A$620 million, which RBC says are higher than consensus expectations. Ramsay's share price fall on Thursday virtually erases all gains since hitting a low in mid-April. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2044 ET - Pharmacy group Ebos's disappointing FY 2025 result costs it a bull. Forsyth Barr downgrades Ebos to neutral from outperform, and cuts its price target by 19% to NZ$37.25 after making material reductions to its earnings forecasts and projecting higher capex. Analyst Matt Montgomerie says the drivers of Ebos's softer-than-expected result and outlook commentary don't appear to be structural. Forsyth Barr still sees Ebos as positioned for medium-term growth. "But at 23x our revised 12-month forward EPS with a high single-digit three-year forward EPS compound annual growth rate, we don't see valuation upside to retain outperform," Forsyth Barr says. Ebos is down 5.5% at NZ$32.00. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1323 ET - Pfizer and BioNTech say they will immediately begin shipping their updated Covid-19 vaccine to pharmacies, hospitals and clinics across the U.S. for use this fall. The vaccine, which received FDA approval Wednesday, targets the LP.8.1 variant--one of the most recently circulating novel coronavirus lineages. It has been approved for use in adults ages 65 and up, as well as for individuals between the ages of five and 64 who have an underlying condition that puts them at risk for severe outcomes from Covid-19. The U.S. approval comes just over a week since Pfizer and BioNTech received approval for the updated vaccine in Canada. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1005 ET - Falling mortgage rates have pushed the median U.S. monthly mortgage payment down to $2,616, its lowest level since the start of the year, according to Redfin. The weekly average mortgage rate is sitting at 6.58%, its lowest level in 10 months. Lower housing costs are bringing some house hunters off the sidelines. Pending home sales rose 1.6% year over year during the four weeks ending Wednesday. That marks two straight months of increases in pending sales after they fell for most of 2025. But many buyers are still waiting on the sidelines. A lot of prospective buyers are waiting to see if the Fed cuts interest rates in September to buy. But Redfin economists say today's mortgage rates likely already reflect the upcoming rate cut. On the selling side, new listings are up 1.9% year over year. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0740 ET - Switzerland's midcap insurers first-half results aren't set to be particularly exciting, though the subsector could still benefit from strong currency trends, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a note. Helvetia and Baloise--which recently agreed to merge--and Swiss Life will publish their results in September. Swiss primary insurers have had good performances since the start of the year, and their shares have outperformed the Stoxx 600 insurance sub-index, analyst Michele Ballatore writes. Swiss Life's results could see some weakness based on a strong comparative, while the focus for Helvetia and Baloise will be on their upcoming integration, he adds. "Operationally, it should be a relatively benign quarter in [property and casualty] with some headwinds from FX," Ballatore says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0500 ET - Santander's return on tangible equity could reach 20%, Morgan Stanley says in a research note. The Spanish bank could unveil a more than 18% ROTE target at its February investor day, analyst Alvaro Serrano writes. Thanks to the integration of TSB in the U.K., the deployment of its Gravity digital banking platform, and the normalization of its Brazil earnings as the U.S. Federal Reserve has opened the door to lower interest rates, the analyst sees a path to a ROTE of 18% to 19%. This could increase by a further percentage point if the profitability of the bank's U.S. retail operations is addressed, he adds. Shares have risen 84% since the start of the year. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0449 ET - PPF's decision to sell its stake in German broadcaster ProSiebenSat.1 Media supports consolidation efforts, Equita analyst Milo Silvestre writes in a note. The Czech investment conglomerate is tendering around 15.7% of ProSiebenSat.1's share capital into Netherlands-based media broadcasting group MFE-MediaForEurope's $2.4 billion takeover offer. PPF's participation in the takeover bid is supportive for MFE's stock, giving MFE control of ProSiebenSat.1 and removing uncertainty over the asset's future, he says. The consolidation is also strategically sound, strengthening MFE's presence in one of Europe's largest free-to-air advertising markets, he adds. MFE and ProSiebenSat.1 shares rise 6.4% and 2.1%, respectively. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0437 ET - Bitcoin exchange-traded funds are seeing improved demand after recent capital outflows, Deus X Pay director James Madden says. Spot bitcoin ETFs from Fidelity and BlackRock have broken their outflow streak, bringing in $219 million in daily inflows, he says. "This shift reflects a renewed interest in bitcoin, demonstrating that these funds are gaining traction after a challenging period," Madden says. The influx of capital into these ETFs signals confidence in bitcoin's long-term potential and reinforces the narrative of institutional adoption of cryptocurrencies, he says. Bitcoin rises 0.7% to $113,225, recovering after hitting a nearly seven-week low of $108,829 Tuesday, LSEG data show. However, it remains well below the record high of $124,480 reached on August 14.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

2337 ET - Hong Leong Bank's earnings momentum is expected to continue, supported by solid fundamentals and a reduced reliance associate contributions, according to RHB IB analysts Nabil Thoo and David Chong. They note management's net interest margin guidance of 1.8%-1.9% for FY 2026 after factoring in the policy rate cut in July and tighter liquidity. Management also sees opportunities to monetize its fixed-income securities to cushion the earnings downside, they say in a note. The lender is optimistic about the outlook for China associate Bank of Chengdu while remaining open to potential value-unlocking opportunities, they add. RHB raises the target price to MYR25.70 from MYR24.30 with a buy rating on the stock, a top sector pick. Shares rise 3.2% to MYR20.38. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0844 ET - Oil futures are slightly lower with the market focused on Russia-Ukraine and expectations for looser market balances after the summer. Russia-related tariffs and sanctions, along with attacks on Russian oil facilities are keeping crude prices resilient "despite the looming oversupply that should lower prices, particularly after the Mideast summer burn for cooling purposes and the end of the summer driving season," Anthony Yuen of Citi Research says in a note. He attributes that to OPEC+ output increases being less than expected, while "the amount of Indian diversification away from Russian oil purchases could also be greater than reported, as reflected in a much tighter Brent-Dubai spread." Citi keeps its average 3Q price forecast for Brent at $66 a barrel, and sees the benchmark averaging $63 in 4Q as "oversupply should start to exert greater impact on the oil supply-demand balance into year end." Brent is off 0.3% at $67.87 and WTI is off 0.2% at $64.04.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0635 ET - Drax's agreement with the U.K. government to keep its North Yorkshire power plant open after 2027 through the use of subsidies could face additional political scrutiny, Jefferies analysts write. This is after the Financial Conduct Authority launched an investigation into certain historical statements regarding its biomass sourcing and the compliance of some annual reports. The investigation appears to be at a very early stage, which means limited information is currently available, the analysts write. Shares trade down 9% at 630.50 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0539 ET - Orsted's shares will remain under pressure over the near term as it battles uncertainty in the U.S. over its Revolution Wind project and undertakes a rights issue, Berenberg analysts write. However, under all scenarios, including the unlikely situation that it can't divest assets and is forced to cancel its U.S. offshore projects, the Danish energy company will be able to cover capital expenditure through 2027, they write. This is because of its planned $9.4 billion rights issue, they add. A share price of 172 Danish krone is seen as a floor in its valuation, they write. The analysts upgrade the stock's rating to buy, from hold, but with a reduced target price of 225 krone, from 250 krone. Shares trade up 1.9% at 192.65 krone. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0448 ET - Shares in power producer Drax trade lower after the U.K.'s financial watchdog started an investigation into the company's biomass sourcing and recent annual reports. The probe could increase uncertainty over an agreement with the U.K. government to extend the company's power station, RBC Capital Markets analysts say. There is no indication on why it was launched or when it will conclude, the analysts say. Shares trade down 9% at 640 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0316 ET - Cnooc's shares could rise given its breakthroughs in oil and gas exploration and discoveries, Citi's Desmond Law and Oscar Yee say in a note. The Chinese energy company made five new offshore discoveries in China and appraised several large- and medium-sized oil and gas bearing structures in 1H. It looks poised to meet its 2025 target reserve replacement ratio of not less than 130%, they say. Cnooc also advanced its deep water exploration campaign in Guyana and signed its first oil contract for exploration in a new Kazakhstan block, which enlarges its overseas exploration portfolio. Citi maintains its buy rating and HK$24.00 target. Cnooc's Hong Kong-listed shares gain 4.1% to HK$19.41. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0135 ET - QL Resources' earnings are expected to recover in 2H FY2026, supported by its palm oil and clean energy segment via robust solar demand, CIMB Securities analyst Walter Aw Lik Hsin says in a note. A rebound in the marine-product manufacturing segment given a low FY2025 base on softer surimi prices and firmer export demand, as well as improved convenience-store performance through marketing initiatives, are also likely to support the earnings outlook, he says. For its integrated livestock farming segment, the removal of government subsidies for chicken eggs may weigh on profits, but higher egg supply, price adjustments, and margin gains from a stronger ringgit should partly offset the impact, he adds. CIMB cuts QL's target price to MYR4.50 from MYR4.85, while maintaining a hold rating on the stock. Shares are 0.2% higher at MYR4.16. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2102 ET - Karoon Energy could unlock US$200 million-US$300 million of value from the Neon oil field offshore Brazil if it sells a 40% interest, says Citi. Karoon plans to open a data room to potential investors in Neon next month. A deal could reduce the capex risk from developing a deposit that won't come online until 2029, analyst Tom Wallington says. It would also add balance-sheet flexibility from 2H 2026. Citi includes the Neon sell-down among a range of catalysts that support its buy call on the stock. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2040 ET - Meridian Energy's FY 2025 result was terrible but it's highly unlikely to repeat this in the new fiscal year, Forsyth Barr says. More so if Meridian is successful in obtaining access to contingent hydro storage that currently can only be accessed in extreme situations, analyst Andrew Harvey-Green says. Forsyth Barr upgrades Meridian to outperform, from neutral. Meridian held its dividend flat at NZ$0.21/share in FY 2025, but noted the impact this had on its balance sheet. "That said, the risk of a dividend cut is low," Forsyth Barr says. Meridian's net debt-to-Ebitdaf ratio is likely to be 1.5X at the end of FY 2026. "Under normal circumstances Meridian has plenty of balance-sheet capacity," Forsyth Barr says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1523 ET - Oil futures recover part of the previous day's losses, helped by a second straight weekly draw in U.S. crude oil inventories and geopolitical risk. Additional U.S. tariffs on Indian goods went into effect over its purchases of Russian oil, which are continuing. "To boil it down to simple economics, wider discounts on Ural grades relative to higher Middle East grades have outweighed tariff pressures and those discounts have reinvigorated the Indian buying interest," Ben Hoff of Societe Generale says in a note. He notes reports that China stepped in to buy Russian barrels when Indian demand dropped for several weeks. "The tariff uncertainty has, from an oil perspective, done absolutely nothing to change global balances, but has simply shifted trade flows," Hoff adds. WTI settles up 1.4% at $64.15, and Brent rises 1.2% to $68.05.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1352 ET - So-called distributed solar installations, which serve local customers and are typically smaller than utility power plants, can be built faster than other types of renewable-energy projects, giving them an advantage to secure tax credits, says Benjamin Baker, managing partner at GDEV, an investment firm in New York that backs distributed-project developers. The recently passed One Big Beautiful Act accelerated the phase out of tax credits for solar projects. To qualify for the incentives, such projects now have to begin construction by July next year or come online by the end of 2027. "It's a race to put steel in the ground. How many projects can you build by that time?" Baker says. "Distributed projects have much faster development cycles and much faster construction cycles, and so we can be much faster to execute our business plans." (luis.garcia@wsj.com; @lhvgarcia)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0838 ET - Sibanye Stillwater delivered a relatively strong set of first-half results with Ebitda and free cash flow beating consensus expectations, RBC Capital Markets analysts write. A positive element was management appearing to open the door to delaying the commissioning and production at its Keliber plant given the current lithium market conditions, they say. This suggests that the South African precious-metals miner is willing to restructure low-margin operations, they say. The company's exposure to gold and platinum group metals, combined with the potential for a portfolio restructure under its new management, should drive a re-rating of its shares. Shares trade down 2.2% at 3,402 rand ($192). (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0500 ET - Hochschild Mining is unlikely to get full operations up and running at its Mara Rosa mine in Brazil until the second half 2026, Berenberg analysts write. Despite previously flagging woes at the open-pit gold mine, Wednesday's production guidance cut and raised cost expectations were more severe than expected and missed consensus expectations, they add. Investors will likely welcome an updated long-term plan for the mine in 2026 once the operational outlook is clearer, the analysts write. Operational challenges aren't the only headwind the miner faces, with inflation in Argentina pushing costs higher, they write. Shares trade down 2.2% at 271.80 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

2245 ET - Palm oil futures fall in Asia trade, tracking weaker soybean oil on the Chicago Board of Trade, says David Ng, a trader at Kuala Lumpur-based Iceberg X. Rising palm oil output is also weighing on market sentiment in the crude palm oil market, he says. The Malaysian Palm Oil Association reported a 3.0% on-month increase in CPO production for Aug. 1-20. Ng expects support for CPO futures at 4,400 ringgit a ton and resistance at 4,580 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for October delivery is down MYR2 at MYR4,486 a ton. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2230 ET - Rapidly improved rare-earths market conditions have allowed Lynas to tap equity markets to support growth plans to 2030, Jefferies analyst Mitch Ryan says in a note. "Securing strategic new offtakes or downstream partnerships would support [its] current valuation," says Ryan, who adds that Lynas is well-positioned to achieve that. He says Lynas's FY 2025 profit results are stronger than expectations. Jefferies is positive on its medium- to long-term outlook, with Lynas strategically placed to take advantage of strength in the rare-earths market, Ryan says. Jefferies has a buy rating and a A$12.50 target on Lynas. Shares are halted and last traded at A$14.73. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2027 ET - South32's portfolio continues to pivot toward base metals, "with Hermosa remaining the core long-term valuation anchor," Jefferies analyst Mitch Ryan says in a note following the miner's FY profit result. South32 missed profit expectations, and its dividend was also a tad light, Ryan notes. FY 2026 guidance also points to higher operating costs across most operations, he says. Jefferies has a hold rating and A$3.10 target on South32. The stock is down 3.1% at A$2.82. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2020 ET - South32's FY 2025 underlying profit is 5% below consensus and its FY 2026 cost forecasts are higher than envisaged, a modest negative, says Citi analyst Paul McTaggart. "Unit cost guidance for FY26 shows modest cost inflation," he says. Underlying Ebitda and net cash are in line with expectations, McTaggart adds. Citi has a neutral rating and A$3.20 target on South32. The stock is down 2.1% at A$2.85. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0808 GMT - HD Hyundai Heavy Industries could see a surge in backlog orders, revenue and operating profit after a planned merger with smaller shipbuilder HD Hyundai Mipo, Nomura analysts Eon Hwang and Heesoo Min write in a research note. The two South Korean shipmakers plan to merge into one entity through a share-for-share exchange by December, with HD Hyundai Heavy Industries set to become the continuing entity. The analysts expect HD Hyundai Heavy's post-merger backlog orders to jump 31% to $51 billion. They also expect HD Hyundai Heavy's revenue to climb 30% to KRW22 trillion and operating profit to increase 20% to KRW2.3 trillion after the merger. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0035 GMT - Jefferies analysts and Qantas bulls Anthony Moulder and Amit Kanwatia say the outlook for Australia's largest carrier is looking positive. The Jefferies analysts say the airline's FY 2025 underlying profit before tax result was slightly ahead of market expectations, and that travel demand is expected to remain strong through 1H FY 2026. That will support higher capacity growth than expected, they note. Qantas said earlier today that its FY 2025 underlying profit before tax rose some 15%. Investors seem pleased, with Qantas shares rising 13% in recent trade. (mike.cherney@wsj.com)

1734 GMT - The new-vehicle sales pace in August is expected to be higher than in August 2024, up to almost 16 million from last year's 15.1 million level, Cox Automotive said. August sales are seen reaching 1.46 million, an increase of 2.3% over August 2024, Cox said. The electric-vehicle market saw significant growth in July, a trend likely continued into August as consumers sought to buy before the expiration of federal tax credits, Cox added. However, the new-vehicle market is likely to slow in the fourth quarter on moderating EV sales, higher prices and tighter inventory, according to Cox. Charlie Chesbrough, senior economist at Cox Automotive, said, "Sales of EVs will likely fall dramatically when tax credits expire, and market conditions for other vehicles will become more challenging in future months." (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

data.title: UK stocks close lower as tech, utility shares drag
data.description:
Key points:
  • FTSE 100 down 0.4%, FTSE 250 slips 0.3%
  • Drax Group slumps after financial watchdog probe
  • PPHE Hotel drops after HY earnings
  • IT firm Softcat gains on annual forecast lift

London equities ended lower for a third consecutive session on Thursday, with utilities and technology shares dragging on the main indexes, as investors assessed chip giant Nvidia's results.

The blue-chip FTSE 100 CURRENCYCOM:UK100 finished 0.4% lower, while the domestically focussed midcap index shed 0.3%.

The FTSE 100 had touched a record high last week when global markets got a lift after U.S. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell signalled a possible interest rate cut at the central bank's September meeting.

Drax Group fell 7.5% after Britain's financial watchdog investigated the energy company's biomass sourcing disclosures.

Other utility stocks (.FTUB6510) were also under pressure, with Centrica and SSE both down 1.5%.

The technology index (.FTUB1010) shed 1.4%, tracking global moves, after results from AI bellwether Nvidia fell short of some analysts' expectations.

Precious metal mining stocks dipped, with Hochschild and Endeavour Mining both falling around 2%.

Losses were held in check by industrial mining stocks, with miners Anglo American and Rio Tinto gaining 2.9% and 1.5%, respectively.

Among other individual stocks, PPHE Hotel was the top decliner on the FTSE 250, slumping 16.2% after the hospitality group posted lower half-yearly earnings.

GSK slipped 1.2% despite Britain's medical regulator approving the drugmaker's new antibiotic pill Blujepa for treating urinary tract infections in women.

IT firm Softcat was the top gainer on the midcap index, adding 3.8% after lifting its annual profit growth forecast for the third time in six months.

The Confederation of British Industry said that services businesses reported a fall in confidence and activity this month, with the employers' group urging finance minister Rachel Reeves not to increase the corporate tax burden.

The Fund is managed with a goal of generating as much of the distribution as possible from net ordinary income and short-term capital gains, that is consistent with the Fund's investment strategy and risk profile. To the extent that sufficient distributable income is not available on a monthly basis, the Fund will distribute long-term capital gains and/or return of capital in order to maintain its managed distribution rate. A return of capital may occur, for example, when some or all of the money that was invested in the Fund is paid back to shareholders. A return of capital distribution does not necessarily reflect the Fund's investment performance and should not be confused with "yield" or "income". Even though the Fund may realize current year capital gains, such gains may be offset, in whole or in part, by the Fund's capital loss carryovers from prior years.

The Board may amend the terms of the Plan or terminate the Plan at any time without prior notice to the Fund's shareholders. The amendment or termination of the Plan could have an adverse effect on the market price of the Fund's common shares. The Plan will be subject to the periodic review by the Board, including a yearly review of the fixed rate to determine if an adjustment should be made.

Notes:

1.

Rule 8 of the Takeover Code (the "Code") sets out the circumstances in which Dealing Disclosures and/or Opening Position Disclosures are required to be made by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them, persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more and exempt principal traders ("EPTs") connected with the parties to an offer.

2.

Where an offeror is marked "Disclosure of dealings and positions in this offeror is not required", dealings and positions in relevant securities of that offeror are not required to be disclosed under Rule 8. This is either because it has been announced that the offer or possible offer is, or is likely to be, solely in cash or because disclosure is not required for some other reason.

3.

Dealings and positions required to be disclosed under Rule 8 of the Code include dealings and positions in derivatives referenced to, and options in respect of, relevant securities of an offeree company and, where appropriate, an offeror (see Note 2 above).

4.

The information set out in the Disclosure Table relating to the relevant securities in issue of an offeree company or of an offeror (and any amendments thereto) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by such company under Rule 2.9 of the Code. Similarly, details of any deadline set under Rule 2.6 by which a potential offeror must announce either a firm intention to make a bid or that it does not intend to make an offer (or, in the case of Rule 2.6(a), by which the Panel must consent to an extension of the deadline) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by the offeree company.

5.

Additions and deletions of offeree companies and offerors are reflected in the Disclosure Table throughout the day, following the relevant announcement. Other amendments may also be made throughout the day. In any event, details of the classes of relevant securities issued by an offeree or offeror company, the number of such securities in issue ("NSI") and the International Securities Identification Number ("ISIN") of such securities announced under Rule 2.9 of the Code before 5.00 pm each day will be included in the final version of the Disclosure Table by 5.30 pm that day. The final version of the Disclosure Table issued each day will be marked "Final" and persons required to make Dealing Disclosures and Opening Position Disclosures are advised to use that final version. Any changes to the Rule 2.6 deadline of a potential offeror will also be reflected in the "Final" version of the Disclosure Table, although any such changes will not be shown in the "Amendments" section at the top of the Disclosure Table.

6.

Dealing Disclosures by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them and connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the business day (as defined in the Code) following the date of the relevant dealing and Dealing Disclosures by persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by 3.30 pm on the business day following the date of the relevant dealing.

7.

Opening Position Disclosures by the parties to an offer and by connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Where required, Opening Position Disclosures by persons with interests in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by no later than 3.30 pm on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Details of the time and date at which an offer period commenced and at which an offeror was first identified are set out in the Disclosure Table. In order to calculate the deadline for Opening Position Disclosures, it is therefore necessary to count forward 10 business days from the relevant date.

8.

Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a Regulatory Information Service ("RIS").

9.

This Note is applicable if it says "See Note 9 below" by the name of the company in the Disclosure Table above.  The Executive has granted the company a dispensation from the requirements under the Code that announcements must be published via a RIS. The company is instead required to publish all announcements on its website. The Executive has also granted a dispensation from Note 3 on Rule 8 of the Code that disclosures made under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a RIS. Therefore, Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code may be made to the company at the address stated in the announcement that commenced the offer period.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1211 ET - Gold futures rise on mounting bets of a September U.S. interest-rate cut. Futures are up 0.55% at $3,467.70 a troy ounce. The precious metal has made steady gains since Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's speech last Friday, in which he opened the door to easing monetary policy, Trade Nation's David Morrison says in a note. Investors are also keeping a close eye on the weaker U.S. dollar, which is currently influencing gold prices to an extent, Morrison says. A weaker dollar is less competitive with gold's safe-haven aspects and makes it cheaper for international investors to buy dollar-denominated bullion. In regards to the dollar, upcoming data releases including core personal consumption expenditure data on Friday should keep the market on alert, Morrison says. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1132 ET - Grains futures are broadly lower, led by wheat. "Russia is still being watched for dry weather that has so far hurt yields and Ukraine is watched for the same reason, and because of the war that could destroy some fields," Jack Scoville of the Price Futures Group says in a note. But while Black Sea prices have been firming as producers hold back sales to compensate for yield losses, "reports from Russia indicate that later weather has improved and that somewhat higher yields than were forecast earlier are now possible." Southern hemisphere crops appear to be good, he adds. CBOT wheat is off 0.6%, corn slips 0.2% and soybeans are down 0.4%. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1103 ET - U.S. natural gas futures press higher after an EIA report showing a smaller-than-expected injection into storage for last week, reducing the surplus over the five-year average. Natural gas inventories rose by 18 billion cubic feet to 3,217 Bcf. The injection was below the 28 Bcf estimate in a Wall Street Journal survey of analysts, and smaller than the 38 Bcf five-year average build for the week. Gas in storage stood 154 Bcf above the five-year average and 112 Bcf below the year-earlier level. Nymex natural gas is up 2.9% at $2.969/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1026 ET - Livestock futures are mixed with lean hogs extending their recovery to five sessions and live cattle holding around record highs. The USDA estimated week-to-date hog slaughter through Wednesday at 1.42 million head, which was down from 1.44 million the previous week and 1.43 million in the year-earlier period. Pork cutout prices were mostly lower. Lean hog futures on CME are up 0.1% at 93.975 cents a pound. Live cattle are off 0.1% at $2.4305 a pound. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1019 ET - Base metal prices rise on a weaker U.S. dollar, with LME three-month copper up 0.5% at $9,821.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum up 0.4% at $2,614.50 a ton. Metal prices have gained in relatively thin trading as a weaker dollar makes it cheaper for international purchasers to buy dollar-denominated commodities. However, there are emerging signs of overall weaker Chinese demand over the second half of 2025, JPMorgan analysts say in a note. Grid investment growth in China declined in July following a continued sharp slowdown in renewables construction, and while automobile production was resilient in July, it is likely to begin decelerating, JPM says. After strong frontloading over the first half, implied Chinese copper demand growth contracted on-year in July, and weaker downstream Chinese aluminum demand is driving up inventories, JPM adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0944 ET - Grains futures are slightly lower in light trade after the USDA reports export sales for last week. "New crop beans finally had a better export report," Doug Bergman of RCM Alternatives says in a note. Soybean sales were 1.18 million metric tons, while corn sales totaled 2.07 million tons and wheat sales 579,800 tons. "New crop corn was very good again," Bergman says. "Old crop corn and bean cancellations are normal for this time of year." Soybeans and corn are off 0.2%, and wheat is down 0.4%. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0934 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are slightly higher, adding to the previous session's gains as the October contract moves to the front of the curve. "Yesterday's rally increases the stakes for this morning's EIA storage report," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics says in a note. "A second straight bullish EIA surprise may extend yesterday's relief rally." Analysts in a Wall Street Journal survey expect a storage build of 28 Bcf for last week, which would reduce the surplus over the five-year average to 164 billion cubic feet from 174 Bcf the week before. Nymex natural gas is up 0.4% at $2.897/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0844 ET - Oil futures are slightly lower with the market focused on Russia-Ukraine and expectations for looser market balances after the summer. Russia-related tariffs and sanctions, along with attacks on Russian oil facilities are keeping crude prices resilient "despite the looming oversupply that should lower prices, particularly after the Mideast summer burn for cooling purposes and the end of the summer driving season," Anthony Yuen of Citi Research says in a note. He attributes that to OPEC+ output increases being less than expected, while "the amount of Indian diversification away from Russian oil purchases could also be greater than reported, as reflected in a much tighter Brent-Dubai spread." Citi keeps its average 3Q price forecast for Brent at $66 a barrel, and sees the benchmark averaging $63 in 4Q as "oversupply should start to exert greater impact on the oil supply-demand balance into year end." Brent is off 0.3% at $67.87 and WTI is off 0.2% at $64.04.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0732 ET - Gold futures rise on a weaker U.S. dollar, safe-haven demand and mounting expectations of a September U.S. interest-rate cut. Futures are up 0.4% at $3,461.10 a troy ounce. The precious metal has gained as expectations of looser monetary policy raise the appeal of non-interest bearing bullion, SP Angel analysts say in a note. Market focus will be on Friday's Personal Consumption Expenditure data, with concerns centering around the interplay between unemployment and containing inflation, SP Angel says. The market currently expects an 88% chance of a 25 basis-point rate cut in September. Investor concerns over the Federal Reserve's independence from the White House following President Trump's move to oust Governor Lisa Cook might be feeding gold's rally, though steady gains in U.S. Treasuries suggest fears aren't yet overbearing, SP Angel adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0625 ET - Base metal prices rise in muted trading, with LME three-month copper up 0.2% at $9,796.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum up 0.2% at $2,608.50 a ton. Base metal prices have inched higher on a weaker U.S. dollar, as it becomes cheaper for international purchasers to buy dollar-denominated commodities. Still, copper trading volumes continue to slide as underlying prices remain broadly rangebound, Britannia Global Markets' Neil Welsh says in a note. This reflects muted industrial demand from China and cautious global growth sentiment, coupled with easing supply-side concerns on improved physical output from key producers, Welsh says. Major aluminum producers meanwhile expect supply constraints to bolster prices, as China caps national aluminum smelting capacity at 45 million metric tons a year to rein in power consumption and emissions, Welsh adds.(joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0613 ET - Palm oil futures ended lower, tracking lower soybean oil prices overnight. There is easing demand from most key buyers except China--as elevated prices and the narrowing discount to soy oil likely dampened sentiment, Kenanga Futures writes in a note. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives contract for November delivery fell 40 ringgit to 4,448 ringgit a ton. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0355 ET - Gold futures trade sideways, holding firm at higher prices on concerns about U.S. Federal Reserve independence. Futures are flat on $3,449.70 a troy ounce, its highest level in more than two weeks. The precious metal is finding support on investor unease over Fed developments and potential inflation risks in the U.S., MUFG analysts say in a note. Fed Governor Lisa Cook has pledged to contest President Trump's attempt to oust her over alleged mortgage document falsification. If Trump's attempt to remove Cook is successful, it would give him a majority on the seven-member Fed board and heighten concerns about political influence over monetary policy, raising safe-haven demand for gold, MUFG says. Investors are concerns that reduced Fed independence could prompt premature interest-rate cuts, eroding market confidence and fueling inflation, analysts add. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

data.title: 'Jay Kelly' sees George Clooney typecast as Hollywood star
data.description:
Key points:
  • Film blends humour with reflection on cost of fame
  • 'Jay Kelly' competes for Venice's Golden Lion prize
  • Clooney absent, skips press event due to sinus infection

George Clooney plays to type in his latest movie "Jay Kelly", which premieres at the Venice Film Festival on Thursday, taking on the role of a Hollywood legend who has to count the cost of a lifetime in the spotlight.

U.S. director Noah Baumbach told reporters that as he worked on the project with British actor-writer Emily Mortimer, he realised the role was tailor-made for the charismatic Clooney.

"Early on, we just started to say, this is going to be George," Baumbach told reporters, saying it was important for the lead actor to be a global superstar whom audiences could instantly relate to, like the character on the screen.

Clooney has a long relationship with Venice, making his first appearance at the world's oldest film festival 27 years ago. However, he missed Thursday's press conference after coming down with what organisers described as a severe sinus infection.

"Even movie stars get sick," said Baumbach, who is entering the main Venice competition for the third time after "Marriage Story" in 2019 and "White Noise" in 2022.

The film tells the story of a celebrated actor stricken by self-doubt while journeying across Europe with his devoted manager, played by Adam Sandler, as the rest of his entourage melts away, including his publicist portrayed by Laura Dern.

The movie weaves sharp humour with poignant self-reflection as the protagonists grapple with lingering grudges, missed opportunities and fractured family ties, pushing them all to confront the compromises and choices that shaped their lives.

"When you read a script like this, you say, Holy shit, I can't believe that I'm getting this gift," said Sandler.

The film shows Clooney's character demanding enormous sacrifices from his army of helpers, expecting them to subordinate their lives to his whims without hesitation.

Sandler and Dern said the film made them appreciate even more their own backstage staff. "(Do) I know if my publicist has a family? I definitely did, but I definitely want to be that much more mindful now," said Dern.

"Jay Kelly" is one of 21 films competing for the prestigious Golden Lion prize, which will be awarded on September 6.

The picture is one of three films put forward by giant streamer Netflix. The other two, Guillermo del Toro's "Frankenstein" and Kathryn Bigelow's "A House of Dynamite", will be shown later in the festival.
